text: The surgeon places trocars and robotic instruments, then begins the operation by dissecting the peritoneum surrounding the cystic duct and artery to isolate them before gallbladder resection.
The surgeon isolates the cystic artery, which branches from the right hepatic artery and supplies blood to the gallbladder.

The surgeon isolates the cystic artery, which is seen going directly to the gallbladder. Tiny branches are visible, requiring separation, cauterization, or clipping. The surgeon places a couple of clips on the cystic artery.
The surgeon uses special clips to securely lock around the cystic artery, ensuring hemostasis and preventing bleeding after the gallbladder resection.

Following the ligation and division of the cystic artery, the surgeon continues to separate the gallbladder from the liver using robotic instruments. A small branch is identified, clipped, and divided before proceeding with the dissection.

The surgeon aims to separate the gallbladder from the main structures, including the hepatic artery and common bile duct. The gallbladder is attached to these structures by a small point known as the cystic duct.

The surgeon has created a window known as the triangle of safety, ensuring the cystic duct is directly connected to the gallbladder and separated from the main structures, including the hepatic artery and common bile duct.
The surgeon places several clips around the cystic duct, visibly locking them in place to prevent future bile leakage.

In the right lower portion of the surgical field, the common bile duct and hepatic artery are visible. The surgeon uses cautery to separate the gallbladder from the liver, placing it into a plastic pouch for removal through a small incision.
